era_context: Late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial and continental power. The Ottoman Empire in slow structural decline. The Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidating personal rule. The Scientific Revolution advancing. Absolute monarchy entrenched in France and Spain. Religious and dynastic conflicts still shaping European politics.

events.title: Treaty of Lisbon recognizes Portuguese independence
events.description: Spain formally recognized Portuguese independence through the Treaty of Lisbon signed on February 13, 1668. This concluded the Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668), which had begun when Portugal rebelled against Spanish rule during the Iberian Union. The treaty was a major diplomatic victory for Portugal and restored its status as an independent kingdom. The recognition came after decades of military conflict and represented a significant shift in the balance of power on the Iberian Peninsula.
key_figures: John IV of Portugal (deceased 1656, but his reign initiated the restoration)
Afonso VI of Portugal (reigning king at treaty)
events.certainty_note: The treaty date and terms are precisely documented in diplomatic records. This is one of the most well-attested events of the year.

events.title: Cretan War continues; Candia under siege
events.description: The siege of Candia (Heraklion) continued throughout 1668, now in its 20th year (begun 1648). The Venetian garrison, under Francesco Morosini, held out against Ottoman forces despite dwindling supplies and reinforcements. The siege would conclude in 1669 with Ottoman victory, but 1668 represented the final year of sustained Venetian resistance. The prolonged siege was one of the longest in early modern warfare and symbolized the declining power of Venice against the Ottoman Empire.

sources.name: Venetian state archives and contemporary siege accounts

events.certainty_note: The siege is extensively documented. The year 1668 is confirmed as part of the ongoing siege.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor removes Oboi regents
events.description: In 1668, the young Kangxi Emperor (then age 14–15) began the process of removing the regents of the Oboi clique, who had dominated Qing governance during his minority. The removal was completed by 1669, but the political maneuvering began in 1668. This marked a crucial turning point in Qing history, establishing Kangxi's personal authority and enabling him to pursue his own policies of Han-Manchu integration and territorial expansion. The removal of regents was a significant political victory for the young emperor.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and the Kangxi Emperor's own writings

events.certainty_note: The process of removing regents occurred over 1667–1669. 1668 marks the beginning of this process, though the final removal was completed in 1669.

events.title: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns intensify
events.description: In 1668, the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb intensified military campaigns in the Deccan, seeking to expand Mughal control over the southern Indian kingdoms. These campaigns would continue for decades and represent a major commitment of Mughal resources. The Deccan campaigns were militarily successful in expanding Mughal territory but also contributed to the strain on the empire's finances and stability that would become apparent after Aurangzeb's death.

sources.name: Mughal court records and contemporary chronicles
Historiographic consensus on Aurangzeb's military campaigns

events.certainty_note: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns are well-documented. The intensification in 1668 is established in Mughal records.

events.title: English East India Company establishes Calcutta
events.description: The English East India Company established a trading post at Calcutta (Kolkata) in 1668, acquiring land from the Mughal authorities. This settlement would grow into one of the most important British colonial centers in India and eventually become the capital of British India. The establishment of Calcutta marked a significant expansion of EIC presence in Bengal and represented the beginning of what would become the center of British power in South Asia.
key_figures: Job Charnock (EIC official, traditionally credited with founding Calcutta, though the date is disputed)

sources.name: East India Company records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The founding of Calcutta is traditionally dated to 1690 or 1686 by some sources, but EIC presence in the area dates to the 1660s. The exact date of formal establishment is debated; 1668 represents early EIC activity in the location.

events.title: Louis XIV's court culture at height
events.description: By 1668, Louis XIV's court at the Louvre and Saint-Germain-des-Prés was at the height of its cultural influence. The king was actively patronizing the arts, literature, and theater. Molière's plays were being performed, and the French court was becoming the model for European aristocratic culture. This period represented the consolidation of French cultural dominance in Europe, which would continue through the reign.
key_figures: Jean-Baptiste Colbert (Minister of Finance and patron of the arts)

sources.name: French court records and contemporary accounts
Published works of Molière and other contemporary writers

events.certainty_note: Louis XIV's cultural patronage and the flourishing of French arts in 1668 are well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Molière's 'L'Avare' premieres
events.description: Molière's comedy 'L'Avare' (The Miser) premiered in Paris in September 1668. The play was an immediate success and remains one of Molière's most celebrated works. It exemplifies the height of French classical comedy and Molière's mastery of character and social satire. The play's success reinforced Molière's position as the leading playwright of the French court and contributed to the cultural dominance of French theater in Europe.

sources.name: Published text of 'L'Avare' and contemporary performance records

events.certainty_note: The premiere date of 'L'Avare' is precisely documented. The play's success is attested in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding date of Calcutta is disputed in historical sources. While 1668 is sometimes cited as the beginning of EIC activity in the area, other sources place the formal establishment at 1690 or 1686. The traditional attribution to Job Charnock is also questioned by modern scholarship, which suggests the settlement developed more gradually. This represents a case where the precise dating of a significant colonial event is less certain than commonly assumed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1668 is rich in European, Ottoman, Qing, and Mughal sources. The record is heavily weighted toward political, military, and cultural events in Europe and Asia. Economic history requires synthesis from multiple sources. Significant gaps exist in detailed knowledge of sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas (outside European colonial contexts), and Southeast Asia for this specific year. The Calcutta founding date illustrates how colonial history can be subject to competing narratives and uncertain dating.

graph_edges.note: Both events represent major geopolitical shifts in 1668: Portuguese independence and the ongoing Cretan War.
The siege of Candia in 1668 led directly to its fall in 1669.
The removal of regents begun in 1668 was completed in 1669, establishing Kangxi's personal rule.
Molière's premiere was part of the broader flourishing of French court culture in 1668.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
